data.title: Genenta Science Shares Double on DNA Technology Platform Collaboration with Anemocyte
data.description:
By Adriano Marchese

Genenta Science shares surged by more than double Friday after the company said it has partnered with Anemocyte to collaborate on its plasmid DNA technology platform.

The stock was briefly halted from trading in the morning and was recently trading just over twofold at $6.83 a share.

The immuno-oncology said it has entered into a strategic collaboration with a focus on an off-the-shelf lentiviral vector plasmid DNA technology platform.

The platform is a ready-to-use system for producing genetic material used in advanced therapies, especially gene and cell therapies.

Genenta's platform will allow Anemocyte, a biotech manufacturing company, to enhance its offering to clients, providing a reliable source of materials for various uses in preclinical to commercial stages.

Write to Adriano Marchese at adriano.marchese@wsj.com

data.title: Procter & Gamble to Focus on Innovation, Not Discounts, to Attract Wary Shoppers — Update
data.description:
By Nicholas G. Miller and Kelly Cloonan

Procter & Gamble reported higher first-quarter sales and said it was investing in product innovation instead of lowering prices to draw cautious consumers.

P&G Chief Financial Officer Andre Schulten said the company was innovating on products ranging from diapers to laundry detergent to combat competitors that are offering aggressive promotions, particularly in the fabric and baby care markets.

The company's product innovation has driven a 2% to 2.5% price increase across the company's entire portfolio, while P&G expects a milder effect from tariffs this fiscal year, Schulten said.

"Consumers are a bit more careful in terms of purchase decisions and consumption. The market gets tighter. And some of the competitive response is increased promotion," Schulten said. "This plan takes longer. It's not as easy as throwing promotion funding out there."

P&G has released Tide's new Evo line of laundry detergent, which Schulten said has led to significant category growth and retailer demand, and made improvements in Pampers diapers and Olay body wash.

"The competitive aggressiveness has increased. And the way we respond is more structural. It takes a bit more time," Schulten said. "While we will remain competitive in the short term, we truly believe the right answer here is to drive integrated superiority with innovation and investment in our brands."

Shares ticked up 1.2% to $153.11. Despite the intraday gains, the stock has fallen 9.3% this year.

The company, known for Crest toothpaste and Pantene shampoo, said first-quarter sales rose 3%, to $22.39 billion, ahead of analysts' forecasts for $22.18 billion, according to FactSet.

Schulten said organic sales grew 5% in Greater China, where the company has earlier used product innovation to combat a challenging consumer environment. "It gives us confidence that these interventions were driving. They take some time, but they ultimately result in what we want in terms of market growth and share growth," Schulten said.

Overall, organic sales edged up 2% in the quarter due to higher pricing and a more favorable mix, with growth in the company's beauty, grooming and healthcare segments.

Schulten said many consumers are trading up and that its premium products have supplied much of the company's growth in some channels.

P&G now expects tariffs to add about $400 million to its annual costs, cutting its prior estimate of $800 million in half. The company also halved its commodity cost outlook to $100 million from $200 million. Both estimates are after tax.

Schulten said the company will see a significant benefit from tariff exemptions the Trump administration is offering on products that can't be naturally produced in the U.S., as well as a decline in retaliatory tariffs.

Looking ahead, P&G continues to forecast sales growth of 1% to 5% and for net earnings per share to climb 3% to 9% for the fiscal year, which ends June 30.

Chief Executive Jon Moeller said P&G is on track to deliver within those ranges despite a challenging consumer and geopolitical environment. The company is increasing its investment in innovation and marketing to boost value and growth, he added.

For the third quarter, the Cincinnati company logged a profit of $4.75 billion, or $1.95 a share, compared with $3.96 billion, or $1.61 a share, a year earlier. The increase was primarily driven by higher noncore restructuring charges in the year-ago quarter, the company said.

Adjusted earnings per share were $1.99. Analysts polled by FactSet expected $1.90 a share.

P&G said organic sales in its beauty segment grew by 6%, helped by new hair-care and personal-care products at higher prices. Organic sales rose 3% in its grooming unit due to new products at higher price points and volume growth, and ticked up 1% in its healthcare segment due to higher prices.

Write to Nicholas G. Miller at nicholas.miller@wsj.com.

data.title: I Bonds' New Rate Is Coming Soon. Here's Where It Could be Set. — Barrons.com
data.description:
Andrew Bary

The new rate on the Treasury's inflation-linked savings bonds is likely to be set at just over 4% for purchases starting in November.

That rate would be little changed from the 3.98% interest rate that has prevailed since May 1.

The Treasury sets new rates on inflation-linked savings bonds, or I bonds, every six months based on the change in the consumer price index. The new rate for the six-month period starting Nov. 1 is likely to be announced by the Treasury in the coming week. Information is available on the Treasury's website.

Barron's estimated the new rate based on the September CPI report released this morning. The new I bond rate formula is based on the six-month change in the non-seasonally adjusted CPI ending in September. Prices rose at a 3.1% annual rate in the period.

To arrive at our 4% estimate, Barron's added a fixed rate of about one percentage point to the annualized CPI gain in the March-September period. I bond rates are based on consumer prices plus a fixed interest rate that remains in place over the life of the bonds.

The fixed rate stood at 1.1% for savings bonds purchased in the current May-October period. That fixed rate in turn tends to follow the real rate on Treasury inflation-protected securities, or TIPS, that trade in the bond market. The current I bond rate of 3.98% is based on the CPI gain over the period from September 2024 to March of this year.

The inflation-linked portion of the interest rate on newly issued bonds will apply for the first six months that an investor holds them. The rate then resets every six months based on the CPI. The fixed rate, however, will prevail over the life of the bonds, which can be held for 30 years.

I bonds can be redeemed after 12 months, but investors lose three months of interest if the bonds are cashed in before five years. I bonds were very popular in 2022, when inflation was running hot. The I bond rate was 9.6% from May through October that year.

I bonds are available through the TreasuryDirect website. Individuals are limited to $10,000 in annual purchases.

Semiannual interest is added to the principal value of the bond, compounding over the life of the bond. This is a favorable feature because it eliminates risk on the reinvestment of interest. In comparison, Treasury notes and bonds make cash interest payments.

Another appealing feature of I bonds is that holders can defer paying taxes on the interest income until they redeem the bonds. This gives I bonds a quality like an individual retirement account.

Interest is exempt from state and local income taxes but subject to federal income tax, which is the same as for Treasury notes and bonds. This makes I bond taxation more favorable than that for bank deposits, whose interest is subject to federal, state, and local income taxes.

Write to Andrew Bary at andrew.bary@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: NY Attorney General Letitia James, a Trump adversary, pleads not guilty to mortgage-related charges
data.description:
Key points:
  • James faces charges of bank fraud and false statement
  • Trump administration targets perceived enemies with criminal charges
  • Federal prosecution of such mortgage cases is rare, Reuters analysis shows

By Andrew Goudsward and Sarah N. Lynch

New York Attorney General Letitia James, a longtime critic of President Donald Trump, pleaded not guilty in federal court on Friday to charges related to allegedly lying on mortgage documents.

James, a Democrat who last year secured a $450 million civil fraud judgment against Trump, is one of three perceived enemies that the Republican president's administration has brought criminal charges against in the past month, along with former FBI Director James Comey and former National Security Adviser John Bolton. All three have denied wrongdoing.

Appearing before a federal judge in Norfolk, Virginia, James said: "Not guilty, judge, to both counts." A trial set was set for Jan. 26, 2026.

Outside of the federal courthouse, supporters of James gathered with signs where they chanted: "Hey hey! Ho ho! Let Letitia James go!" and "This is what democracy looks like."

Trump has promised to seek retribution against people he alleges used the justice system against him in an effort to prevent him from returning to power. He pressured Attorney General Pam Bondi to speed up the action, and replaced the prior U.S. attorney overseeing the James and Comey cases with a loyalist after that lead prosecutor expressed concerns with the strength of the evidence in both matters.

Two other Trump rivals - Democratic Senator Adam Schiff and Federal Reserve Board Governor Lisa Cook - remain under criminal investigation and have also denied wrongdoing.

More than half of Americans, including about one in three Republicans, believe Trump is using federal law enforcement to target his enemies, according to a Reuters/Ipsos poll this month.

TARGETED TRUMP'S ASSETS

James last year won a $454.2 million penalty against Trump after a judge found he fraudulently overstated his net worth to dupe lenders. A New York state appeals court in August threw out the penalty, which had grown to more than $500 million with interest, but upheld the trial judge's finding that Trump was liable for fraud.

Both Trump and James' office are appealing to the state's highest court.

A source familiar with Trump's thinking said that Trump's anger towards James is particularly acute because of the challenges he faced when he struggled to secure a bond to cover the judgment, putting him at risk of having his assets seized.

James became a Justice Department target after Trump ally Bill Pulte, who leads the Federal Housing Finance Agency, made a criminal referral that raised questions about whether James lied on her mortgage applications in order to obtain a more favorable interest rate.

That sort of case is rarely prosecuted by the federal government, a Reuters analysis of eight years of court records showed.

The charges against James focused on a property that she bought in Norfolk, Virginia, in 2020 for around $137,000, not one of the two Pulte initially raised questions about.

The indictment alleges that James falsely told the bank she would occupy the residence as a second home, when she actually used it as an investment property. This alleged misrepresentation allowed her to receive a favorable interest rate that saved her nearly $19,000 over the life of the loan, the indictment says.

James faces two criminal counts, bank fraud and making a false statement to a financial institution.

"We will fight these baseless charges aggressively," she said when the charges were first brought.

James' legal team has already indicated she will seek to have the charges thrown out on the grounds that U.S. Attorney Lindsey Halligan, the prosecutor Trump appointed to lead the case, is unlawfully serving in the role. Comey has made a similar argument in his case.

A hearing on Comey's challenge to Halligan's appointment will be held in Alexandria, VA. on November 13.

That challenge is being heard outside Virginia to avoid any appearance of a conflict of interest.

In an unusual move, an attorney who normally handles civil litigation from a U.S. attorney's office in Missouri has joined Halligan on the case, after prosecutors in Halligan's Virginia office expressed skepticism about the James case.

U.S. District Judge Jamar Walker, who was appointed by former Democratic President Joe Biden, will hear the case.

data.title: Ford surges after Q3 profit beats estimates
data.description:
** Shares of legacy automaker Ford rise 11% to $13.7

** Co posted Q3 earnings per share of 45 cents, beating LSEG analysts' expectation of 36 cents

** Q3 revenue came in at $50.5 bln, up 9% from last year

** Sales of gasoline-fueled trucks and SUVs continue to power profits for the automaker

** However, co cut 2025 forecast for earnings before interest and taxes to a range of $6 bln-$6.5 bln, from $6.5 bln-$7.5 bln earlier

** Piper Sandler says if not for a recent fire at Ford's aluminum supplier, the company would have boosted guidance

** Three of 25 brokerages rate the stock "buy" or higher, 19 "hold" and three "sell" or lower; their median PT is $11 - data compiled by LSEG

** Including session moves, shares up 38.2% YTD

data.title: Porsche's Operating Profit Slumps as EV Challenges, China Woes Take Toll
data.description:
By Nina Kienle

Porsche AG's operating profit plunged in the first nine months of the year as a slow electric-vehicle rollout, weak demand for German premium cars in China and U.S. President Trump's tariffs have taken a toll on the sport-car maker.

The company on Friday posted an operating profit of 40 million euros ($46.5 million), compared with nearly 4.04 billion euros a year earlier, marking a 99% decline. Meanwhile, its operating return on sales slumped to 0.2% from 14.1%.

Porsche is at a critical juncture. Last month, the company warned on profit for the fourth time this year, saying unpredictable demand for EVs had forced it to delay the launch of new all-electric models following a comprehensive review of its portfolio.

The company also said it would add new hybrid and combustion-engine models to its lineup as EV adoption is faster than expected in China but slower elsewhere. Meanwhile, sluggish demand for German premium cars in China and Trump's tariffs have compounded challenges for the carmaker.

Porsche said extraordinary expenses for its realignment efforts amounted to roughly 2.7 billion euros in the first nine months and it expects around 3.1 billion euros in total for the year.

"We expect 2025 to be the trough that precedes a noticeable improvement for Porsche from 2026 onward," Finance chief Jochen Breckner said.

The company is in the middle of a management transition. Last week, it said former McLaren boss Michael Leiters would succeed Oliver Blume as chief executive as of Jan. 1. Blume, CEO of Porsche since October 2015 and of Volkswagen since September 2022, will continue to steer Volkswagen until the end of the decade.

A dual-role arrangement had enabled Blume to stay at the helm of both companies, which are closely connected. Volkswagen owns more than 75% of Porsche AG.

Porsche reported sales of 26.86 billion euros for the first nine months, down 6% on year. It said earlier this month that deliveries had declined 6% to 212,509 vehicles. Out of all vehicles it delivered, Porsche said about 23% were fully electric, while around 12% were plug-in hybrids.

Automotive net cash flow--a closely watched metric by investors--increased to 1.34 billion euros from 1.24 billion euros, lifting Porsche's automotive net cash flow margin to 5.6% from 4.8%. The company said the increase showed its ability to generate cash flow despite the challenges it faces.

For the year, Porsche said it expects sales between 37 billion euros and 38 billion euros, a return on sales of up to 2% and an automotive net cash flow margin between 3% and 5%.

Write to Nina Kienle at nina.kienle@wsj.com

data.title: Terna does not comment report on potential power grid stake sale
data.description:
Italy's power grid operator Terna said on Friday it did not want to comment rumours when asked about a report by Bloomberg newswire hinting at a potential sale of a stake in the country's high-voltage network.

WHY IT'S IMPORTANT

The state-controlled group said in March it would invest more than 23 billion euros ($26.8 billion) in Italy's power grid over the next ten years to help support energy transition.

KEY QUOTE

"Terna does not comment on rumours or speculation: the company constantly analyses the financial instruments available for the development and maintenance of the national transmission grid, the results of which are reflected in the decisions set out in the industrial plan," a spokesperson told Reuters.

CONTEXT

Terna is evaluating options including the sale of a minority stake in Italy's electricity grid, to pay for new projects and reduce debt, Bloomberg reported citing people familiar with the matter.

($1 = 0.8575 euros)

data.title: CAC 40 Sees Flat Daily and Weekly Performance
data.description:
The CAC 40 closed flat at 8,226 on Friday, leaving the index modestly higher for the week after reaching fresh record highs earlier in the week amid rising political tensions and slowing private-sector activity in France.

Socialists warned they could topple the government by Monday unless billionaires face higher taxes, while private-sector activity contracted at its fastest pace since February as both services and manufacturing weakened.

In the United States, September inflation came in below market expectations, reinforcing bets on a Fed rate cut next week.

On the corporate front, Kering tumbled 3.9% after HSBC downgraded the stock to Hold, Vinci fell 2% on signs of slower momentum in airports and motorways, and Safran slipped 1.1% despite raising its 2025 targets following stronger-than-expected third-quarter sales.

In contrast, Accor surged 7% after confirming its full-year 2025 outlook and Sanofi rose 2.5% after beating quarterly forecasts and reaffirming guidance.

data.title: MH Markets CEO Mr. Houimli: Dubai's Branding Strategy Reflects "Global Ambition"
data.description:
DUBAI, UAE / ACCESS Newswire / October 24, 2025 / In a powerful statement of its growth trajectory, MH Markets, a regulated CFD broker and the UAE's leading forex broker, has launched a comprehensive and dominant branding campaign across Dubai, UAE, and its headquarters. The CFD broker in Dubai, a global financial services provider, unveiled larger-than-life billboards on Sheikh Zayed Road near the majestic Museum of the Future, and Mohammed Bin Zayed Road, alongside a strategic fleet branding partnership with taxis operating from Dubai International Airport. This initiative positions MH Markets at the heart of one of the world's most dynamic financial landscapes.

Speaking on the strategic vision behind this significant investment, Mr. Chokri Houimli, CEO (MENA) of MH Markets, stated that the campaign is a direct reflection of the company's core values and future ambitions.

"Our presence in Dubai is not merely about visibility; it is about alignment," Mr. Houimli said. "Much like Dubai itself, MH Markets is built on ambition and vision. By joining the city's iconic skyline, we are expressing that same drive to build a lasting legacy of trust and empower investors right from the heart of this emerging financial hub."

The campaign places MH Markets branding along the city's most futuristic corridors, ensuring engagement with a global audience of investors, entrepreneurs, and financial leaders. This move emphasizes the company's commitment to establishing a profound and lasting connection with the MENA region and beyond.

This branding campaign in Dubai follows another major milestone for the company; in July, a NASDAQ MarketSite feature on the iconic tower in Times Square, New York. During the event, Mr. Houimli made a landmark announcement regarding the company's future, strengthening its status as one of the fastest-growing forex and CFD brokerages in the region.

"Our feature on NASDAQ was a declaration of our global standards and our readiness for the next chapter," Mr. Houimli commented. "It was the perfect platform to announce our preparation for an Initial Public Offering (IPO)in the coming years, a strategic move that will fuel our next phase of innovation and solidify our commitment to transparency for our clients and partners."

By bridging its physical dominance in Dubai with its digital landmark on NASDAQ, MH Markets is crafting a narrative of unparalleled growth and credibility. Mr. Houimli concluded, "This is a synergistic strategy. From the digital pulse of Times Square, to the gigantic billboards sharing Dubai's skyline, to the taxis traversing Dubai's futuristic roads, we are telling a unified story: MH Markets is here to build, to lead, and to empower the modern investor on a global scale."

About MH Markets

MH Markets is a globally recognized leader in financial services, providing foreign exchange and contract-for-difference (CFD) brokerage solutions to clients worldwide. Guided by its commitment to service excellence, innovation, and reliability, the company delivers superior trading environments, personalized support, and a wealth of resources to enhance client experiences.

MH Markets operates under multiple international regulatory frameworks, including the Mauritius FSC, Australia ASIC, Saint Vincent and the Grenadines SVGFSA, Vanuatu VFSC, and the UAE SCA-5, ensuring strict adherence to the highest standards of financial governance.

For more information, please visit: www.mhmarkets.com.

Media Contact:

MH Markets

support@mhmarkets.com

SOURCE: MH Markets

View the original press release on ACCESS Newswire

data.title: Bruton Limited (BRUT) - Final contracts for additional firm and optional VLCC newbuildings
data.description:
Hamilton, Bermuda, 24 October 2025

Reference is made to the stock exchange announcement dated 20 October 2025 by Bruton Limited (Euronext Growth Oslo: BRUT) (the "Company"), announcing that a letter of intent had been agreed between New Times Shipbuilding Co. Ltd. ("NTS") and the Company’s wholly owned subsidiary Andes Tankers III Ltd. ("Andes Tankers III") pursuant to which Andes Tankers III orders two new scrubber fitted VLCC vessels (the “Firm Vessels”) and has options for two additional VLCC vessels at the same price (the “Optional Vessels”) subject to Andes Tankers III declaring the option before 31 December 2025.

Andes Tankers III, its subsidiaries and NTS have now entered into final newbuilding contracts with respect to the Firm Vessels and option agreements with respect to the Optional Vessels.

The Firm Vessels are scheduled for deliveries between Q4 2028 and Q2 2029, whilst the Optional Vessels, if declared, are scheduled for deliveries between Q3 and Q4 2029. All vessels carry the option to be converted to LNG dual fuel ready ships.

This information is considered to be inside information pursuant to the EU Market Abuse Regulation and subject to the disclosure requirements pursuant to section 5-12 of the Norwegian Securities Trading Act. This stock exchange notice was published by Alfi Lao, Contracted Chief Accounting Officer, on the date and time as set out in the release.

About Bruton Limited: Bruton Limited is an industrial player incorporated in Bermuda and seeking to make strategic investments in the shipping, offshore and energy sectors, currently focusing on its VLCC newbuilding program.

data.title: Universal Insurance shares jump 9% on profit rebound in Florida
data.description:
By Keira Wingate

(The Insurer) - Shares of Universal Insurance Holdings rose nearly 9% on Friday after the Fort Lauderdale-based homeowners carrier reported a sharp rebound in profitability, helped by benign weather and improving market conditions in Florida.

The insurer’s combined ratio improved by 20.5 points year-on-year to 96.4%, reflecting a markedly lower loss ratio of 70.2% as the quarter was free of major hurricanes, unlike the prior-year period, which included losses from hurricanes Debby and Helene.

As of 11.30 a.m. EDT (1530 GMT) on Friday, New York-listed Universal's share price was up 7.9% compared to Thursday's closing price of $28.63. The share price was as high as $31.31 earlier on Friday, up 9%.

CEO Stephen Donaghy said the company’s early reserve reviews were “very encouraging” and that Universal is entering a “new chapter in the revamped Florida market”.

Premium trends showed continued stabilization at home and growth elsewhere. Direct premiums written rose 3.2% to $592.8 million, as Florida premiums dipped 2.6% but were offset by a 22.2% jump in other states, underscoring Universal’s push to diversify beyond its core Florida base.

Reinsurance costs remained manageable, with the ceded premium ratio inching up to 32.7% from 31.9% due to added coverage, while net premiums earned increased 4%. Executives said those adjustments strengthen protection heading into 2026 renewals without materially eroding margins.

The company’s strong quarter and calmer catastrophe backdrop have fueled renewed investor confidence in Florida’s recovering homeowners market.

data.title: Big Banks Enter Stablecoin Era by Taking Zelle International — WSJ
data.description:
By Dylan Tokar

Zelle, the payments product owned by the country's largest banks, said Friday that it plans to allow users to start making international payments using stablecoins, a type of cryptocurrency.

The move by Early Warning Services, which is owned by a consortium of banks that includes JPMorgan Chase, Wells Fargo and Citigroup, follows on the heels of legislation championed by the Trump administration designed to usher stablecoins into the regulated financial system.

The details

Zelle on Friday said the initiative would help users move money across borders, and that the service would be available to customers of any bank that is part of the Zelle network. It didn't specify exactly how it would work or when it would launch.

The service would represent a significant expansion for Zelle, which has so far limited its payment services to users in the U.S.

Unlike its domestic payments product, the new initiative would entail the use of stablecoins, a digital currency that is pegged to a fiat currency such as the U.S. dollar. President Trump in July signed a bill that creates federal guardrails and oversight of stablecoins.

Since they are tied to real currency, stablecoins are supposed to maintain a more consistent value than other cryptocurrencies. That makes them particularly attractive as a payments tool, and the new legislation has led to all manner of companies, including Walmart and Amazon, exploring how to issue or use their own coins.

The context

The Wall Street Journal in May reported that the nation's biggest banks were looking into ways to issue a stablecoin, including with Zelle, in a move intended to head off growing competition from the cryptocurrency industry.

Cryptocurrency advocates hope new legislation will foster more widespread adoption of stablecoins, both as a way to make payments and potentially as a way to store value.

Currently, stablecoins are primarily used as an on ramp and off ramp for purchasing other types of cryptocurrency. Stablecoins pegged to the U.S. dollar are also popular in countries where the local currency is subject to inflation.

Cross-border payments is one area in which those in the cryptocurrency and traditional finance industries agree that stablecoins could be particularly useful. Making an intentional payment without them typically involves transferring money across a series of institutions, making it costly and slow.

Write to Dylan Tokar at dylan.tokar@wsj.com

data.title: FTSE 100 Rises to New Record, Climbs 3% this Week
data.description:
The FTSE 100 climbed over 0.5% on Friday to a new record high, extending weekly gains to about 3%, its best performance since April.

The rally was fueled by upbeat UK economic data, as September retail sales strengthened optimism following encouraging public finance figures and easing inflation, signaling resilient consumer activity.

NatWest rose nearly 3% after posting stronger-than-expected profits and raising its 2025 income and returns guidance, underscoring solid business momentum.

The update reflected broader strength across UK banks benefiting from higher-for-longer interest rates.

LSE shares advanced more than 5%, extending a 6.7% surge from the previous session after reporting robust quarterly results.

GSK, however, fell around 1.5% despite US approval of its blood cancer drug Blenrep, with analysts noting regulatory complexities.

Meanwhile, US inflation data showed consumer prices rising slower than expected in September, bolstering global market sentiment.

data.title: UN weather agency reviews priorities as funding falls short
data.description:
Key points:
  • WMO member states owe tens of millions in fees
  • States met in Geneva to discuss disaster warnings
  • US is one of dozens of late payers

By Emma Farge

The U.N. weather agency plans to cut some posts and is reviewing its priorities as dozens of countries, including the United States, are late with their fees, a spokesperson said on Friday.

The World Meteorological Organization, set up in 1951 to coordinate global data for weather forecasts, created a review task force this week during a meeting in Geneva aimed at improving early-warning systems for deadly climate disasters.

The WMO, whose budget is separate from the United Nations', began restructuring in August to cut costs amid broader U.N. reforms, even as accelerating man-made climate change increases the risk of weather-related deaths, especially in developing countries where early-warning systems are lacking.

The WMO envisages cutting 26 posts and reducing travel, according to a budget document seen by Reuters.

"We do need to make sure we are fit for purpose and that we can face the future," spokesperson Clare Nullis told a press briefing on Friday.

She said a task force would begin in January to "tweak" the WMO's work based on current funding constraints as well as new opportunities such as the use of artificial intelligence in weather predictions.

Outstanding late payments to the WMO amount to around 48 million Swiss francs ($60 million) as of the end of August, a WMO document showed, equivalent to two-thirds of its annual budget.

The U.S. owes over 30 million francs.

A U.S. State Department spokesperson said that the administration was focused on ensuring that any U.S. taxpayer dollars at the U.N. advance U.S. interests. U.S. delegates participated in the congress.

Under President Donald Trump, Washington has announced it is quitting some U.N. bodies, as well as the U.N.-backed Paris Climate Accord on slowing climate change, and is late in paying others.

Trump has repeatedly questioned the effectiveness, cost and accountability of international bodies, arguing they often fail to serve U.S. interests.

($1 = 0.7931 Swiss francs)

data.title: Todd Mackay: How Cetera Wants to Help Advisors Accelerate Growth — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Andrew Welsch

For Cetera Financial's Todd Mackay, a key to attracting independent financial advisors is helping them to identify ways of expanding their businesses and then giving them concrete plans for achieving the growth they desire. Those steps aren't dissimilar from the financial planning process that advisors walk clients through, says Mackay, who became president of Cetera Wealth Management in January. "It's about goals and aspirations, and how to achieve them," he says.

Mackay, who oversees recruiting, earlier this year issued public invitations to advisors at Commonwealth Financial Network to join Cetera if they weren't happy about working with LPL Financial, which had agreed to buy Commonwealth. In an interview with Barron's Advisor, Mackay discusses the reasoning behind those pitches, why Cetera can feel simultaneously small and big to its advisors, and how he sees the industry changing in coming years.

Where are you from and how did you get into the industry? I grew up in a family of ranchers and preachers, which is typical in Montana. My dad's generation was the last generation of ranchers. He became a priest and left the ranch, and we grew up in a town. Right out of college, I was in investment banking and focused on wealth management and financial technologies. That led me to working at E*Trade for a long time, which was great. So I've always been in wealth management and financial technology. I joined HD Vest in January 2016, which is now the Avantax business at Cetera.

Did you always think you'd go into wealth management or brokerage? I studied economics in college, with a concentration in math. I always had a passion for data and analytics. To be quite frank, I wasn't sure going into college what I wanted to do. But I knew I wanted to leverage my intellectual curiosity in those areas in the right way. One of the things that intrigued me about wealth management and financial services was the service component.

Tell me about your current role at Cetera. I'm the president of wealth management, which entails being responsible for all of our five channels for our nearly 12,000 advisors and nearly $360 billion in assets under advisement. Within that, I'm responsible for advisor growth productivity, recruiting, retention, and making sure that our advisors have the best partner where they want to take their practice and growth their business.

You were with Avantax, a tax-focused independent broker-dealer, when it was acquired by Cetera. How was the transition? Was that your first experience with an acquisition? Not surprisingly, in the financial services world there has been a lot of consolidation. When I was at HD Vest, we acquired First Global. So I've experienced integrations and seen it from both angles. That's important: to be able to understand both the acquiree and acquirer's side, and not just from the management side, but from the lens of what is best for the advisor and the client.

So I can tell you that from day one of the close of [the Avantax acquisition], we were focused on how we could take advantage of all the things Cetera has to offer, from advisor services to technology. And we were focused on making sure Avantax advisors were aligned with the tax community within Cetera.

Can I ask for an example of how Cetera is a different experience for Avantax advisors? The ones I hear most often from advisors are some of the marketing and growth-related services that Avantax advisors now have access to. That would be the business planning process, which is Growth360 [Cetera's program that helps provide growth plans and support for advisors].

One of the most important things that we do is we engage in consultation with advisors to have a deep understanding about where the advisor is now and where the advisor wants to go. We can't be truly of service until we understand those two things. From a relationship and intensive diagnostic perspective, we have the tools to help them put together comprehensive business plans to achieve what they want to do. It's not too dissimilar to what advisors do with clients to put together a financial plan. It's about goals and aspirations, and how to achieve them. We do the same thing with advisors through our Growth360 tools so that we have a road map for their future success. And then once we have that, we can look in the data for what are their opportunities and inhibitors. And then it's about putting the plan into action. It all boils down to ensuring that we are delivering the right solutions to the right advisors at the right time.

We have invested a lot into the data and diagnostic elements and also in our regional growth teams so that advisors across our channel infrastructure have a dedicated team that is working with them to help them achieve their plan.

Do you see changes in advisor growth? Yes. We have tangible proof points from a data perspective. For those that are engaging in the business planning process and working with our teams to achieve that plan, we see about 20% greater growth than those that haven't engaged in that process. Even more importantly, it helps advisors uncover what's most meaningful to them within their practice. I was talking with an advisor who was in an ensemble practice, had a strong desire for growth, wanted to see 20%-plus growth in terms of new clients and capturing more wallet share. We were able to work with that advisor through their Growth360 plan and help them identify and prioritize their opportunities.

How do you see the independent financial advice business changing over the next couple of years? There are a couple of things that I think are important and tied together. One, attracting new-to-the-industry advisors is an important focus at Cetera. We have a program that does just that in terms of being able to attract, recruit, educate, and retain new advisors. That's important not only for our existing advisors to be able to handle new growth opportunities within their practice, but it also provides a natural succession opportunity.

Advisors entering the financial advice industry today are stepping into a significant opportunity. With more seasoned professionals leaving the field than entering it, there's a growing demand and a wide-open path for new advisors to build thriving businesses and make a meaningful impact in clients' lives.

Technology and artificial intelligence are powerful tools that can help new advisors serve clients more efficiently and scale their practices faster. But while these innovations should be embraced, they're not a substitute for human connection. At its core, financial advice is and will always be a relationship-driven business.

Another big area is succession, and that is tied to the new entrant program. We want to make sure that we have the right options for all of our advisors, whether they want to simply monetize their book and unload the burdens of running a business, or a succession plan where we maybe match them with other advisors. That's important, and we are positioned very well from that perspective.

Another trend in advisor practices is the importance of personalization. Just as we want to make sure that Cetera is providing personalized service to our advisors, it's important for advisors to provide more personalization to their clients. It's coming into play more and more at all levels.

Why is the demand for personalization increasing? You see it not just in our industries, but all industries. Consumers want to be delivered products and services that are targeted to their needs. Whether that's retail or other services, knowing what your client is looking for today and needs in the future — that's not becoming just a nice thing to have, but an expectation.

As president of Cetera Wealth Management, you oversee recruiting. How are the company's efforts to attract advisors going? 2025 is shaping up to be a great year for Cetera from a recruiting perspective. We have one of the largest pipelines in the history of Cetera. Equally important, we are seeing the production of existing advisors increase at record levels. Attrition is very low, and retention is very high. Advisor sentiment is very positive. What I'm hearing from advisors is that they are choosing Cetera for several reasons, including our channel structure. They know where their community is, and they have strong engagement with that community. They like that aspect of being part of something that is smaller but still having access to the resources of a larger organization that is a scaled wealth management business. The other thing I hear is the personalized approach to growth, which is our deep understanding of advisors and where they want to go.

You wrote two open letters to advisors at Commonwealth Financial Network, which was acquired by LPL. Why did you write them and how were those received? We wanted Commonwealth advisors — who are wonderful advisors and have great practices with a client centric approach — to know that there are options out there. Equally important, we wanted them to know that many of the things they enjoyed about Commonwealth over the years are available at Cetera. Our channel architecture can give them a community and a personalized team to support the advisor and their practice.

Another benefit we spotlighted in our letters — and a benefit of the Avantax acquisition — is that we now have access to NFS, Fidelity's clearing and custody platform. [ Editor's note: Fidelity provides the clearing and custody platform for Commonwealth, which means Commonwealth advisors moving to Cetera don't have to go through the often-laborious process of switching custodians.]

data.title: Social Security Announces 2.8% COLA Increase for 2026. That's Less Than Many Retirees Say They Need. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By David Wignall

Retirees and other beneficiaries will see their Social Security benefits increase by 2.8% next year to reflect inflation. The boost will affect more than 70 million people.

The Social Security Administration announced its annual cost-of-living adjustment, or COLA, on Friday. The 2.8% increase will raise the average retirement payment by $56 to an average monthly benefit of $2,071. Most Social Security recipients will see the boost reflected in their January payment.

The 2.8% uptick follows this year's 2.5% increase, the smallest in four years.

A recent poll released by the American Association of Retired Persons found that only 22% of Americans over the age of 50 believed a roughly 3% COLA was sufficient to keep up with rising prices.

More than three-quarters of older adults still face challenges covering basic living expenses, according to the AARP poll.

A 2.8% raise was "apt to look pretty underwhelming to most Social Security recipients," said Mary Johnson, a retired Social Security and Medicare policy analyst, in a statement released on Sept. 11 in anticipation of the relatively small increase. "Much of the COLA, if not all, of that modest increase could be swallowed by Medicare premiums in 2026."

The Bureau of Labor Statistics on Friday said that the Consumer Price Index rose by 3.0% in September compared with a year earlier — slightly cooler than expected but still above the Federal Reserve's target 2.0% rate. The CPI announcement was delayed due to the continuing government shutdown.

By law, the annual COLA is calculated by comparing a version of the CPI for urban workers in July through September compared with a year ago. But the size of the increase has come under criticism for not adequately reflecting the rising cost of living, as expenses like home and health insurance continue to grow.

High inflation is especially dangerous for retirees, since it can lead to higher-than-expected drawdown rates and erodes the purchasing power of savings held in short-term vehicles. Roughly half of the population aged 65 or older live in households that receive the majority of their income from Social Security benefits, according to government data.

"Social Security is a promise kept, and the annual cost-of-living adjustment is one way we are working to make sure benefits reflect today's economic realities and continue to provide a foundation of security," says Social Security Administration Commissioner Frank J. Bisignano.

Future uncertainty. Social Security payments have been rising steadily — and roughly in line with inflation — for decades, thanks to the COLA. But benefits in the future may not be so predictable. The program is careening toward insolvency, thanks to falling birthrates and a growing population of retirees.

The primary trust fund that supports Social Security is expected to run out of money in roughly eight years. When that happens, beneficiaries will have their benefits cut by more than a fifth across the board.

Analysts widely expect Congress to modify the program to prevent insolvency, implementing some combination of tax increases and benefit cuts. But the exact shape of the program after 2034 remains unclear.

"There really is a tremendous amount of uncertainty with your scheduled benefits," says Marc Goldwein, a senior vice president and policy director for the Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget. "If I'm in my 30s, I think there's really a very wide range of possibilities of what my benefits might be."

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: MidWestOne Financial Up Over 34%, On Track for Largest Percent Increase Since March 2003 — Data Talk
data.description:
MidWestOne Financial Group, Inc. ( MOFG ) is currently at $38.17, up $9.80 or 34.55%

  • Would be highest close since Dec. 22, 2016, when it closed at $38.19
  • On pace for largest percent increase since March 28, 2003, when it rose 38.89%
  • Currently up five of the past six days
  • Up 38.26% this week; best weekly performance since the week ending March 28, 2003, when it rose 38.89%
  • Up 34.93% month-to-date; on pace for best month since March 2009, when it rose 38.72%
  • Up 31.09% year-to-date; on pace for best year since 2021, when it rose 32.12%
  • Down 0.85% from its all-time closing high of $38.50 on Dec. 20, 2016
  • Up 35.08% from 52 weeks ago (Oct. 25, 2024), when it closed at $28.26
  • Would be a new 52-week closing high
  • Up 50.34% from its 52-week closing low of $25.39 on April 11, 2025
  • Traded as high as $38.53; highest intraday level since Dec. 24, 2019, when it hit $39.03
  • Up 35.83% at today's intraday high; largest intraday percent increase since Nov. 17, 2003, when it rose as much as 36.8%

All data as of 11:35:46 AM ET

Source: Dow Jones Market Data, FactSet

data.title: BILL Holdings Is Maintained at Outperform by Oppenheimer
VICOR Corp Director Andrew D'Amico Sells All Common Stock Holdings
data.description:
Reporter NameD'Amico Andrew
RelationshipDirector, 10% Owner
TypeSell
Amount$1,262,103
SEC FilingForm 4

Andrew D'Amico, a 10% owner and director of VICOR Corp, has sold a total of 15,242 shares of common stock over transactions dated October 22 and 23, 2025, under a Rule 10b5-1 trading plan adopted on September 12, 2024. The sales were conducted at prices ranging from $80 to $90 per share, totaling $1,262,103. Following these transactions, D'Amico no longer directly owns any common stock in VICOR Corp.

SEC Filing: VICOR CORP [ VICR ] - Form 4 - Oct. 24, 2025

data.title: XRP Ledger Secures $40 Million Tokenization in Brazil With 500% Upside Potential
data.description:
Ripple and XRP Ledger (XRPL) are adding another regulated use case to their achievements — this time in Brazil’s structured credit market.

As became known, São Paulo-based VERT Capital has completed its second tokenized issuance using XRPL and its EVM Sidechain, bringing more than $40 million in pension-backed receivables on-chain.

What this transaction does is expand XRP presence in Latin America, following its use in cross-border payment corridors between Brazil and Portugal earlier this year.

Ripple's building arm, RippleX, has been positioning XRPL as infrastructure not only for payments but also for tokenized assets, all in accordance with the latest trend on the market, with its intentions ranging from stablecoins and real estate in Dubai to money-market funds in the U.S. through RLUSD integration with BlackRock and VanEck.

RippleX
@RippleXDev

Issuer Address: rf15EoP5Z2wR8dK8qFiu3ZwVEeQ8zdXWzr

Oct 24, 2025

Speaking of Brazil, the instrument there is an FIDC — a regulated investment fund that securitizes receivables. The scheme is simple as this issuance is backed by obligations tied to the country’s federal pension system (INSS), considered one of the lowest-risk receivable pools available.

VERT and fintech partner BYX report that the fund currently holds the equivalent of $40 million in assets and could expand another 500% to as much as one billion BRL or $190 million if institutional demand delivers.

Not just tokenization deal

More importantly, though, the partnership also introduced VERT Sign, a blockchain-based signing tool on the XRPL EVM Sidechain. It enables automated, recurring purchases of receivables with full compliance oversight, recording documentation and settlement directly on-chain.

Ripple says Brazil is part of a broader strategy to demonstrate that XRPL can meet regulatory requirements for tokenization. The company and VERT are both involved in the CVM’s LEAP program, a regulatory sandbox designed by Brazil’s securities commission to test blockchain infrastructure on supervised markets.

data.title: Plains All American Price Target Cut to $22.00/Share From $24.00 by Raymond James
